data.title: Tesla: EV Market Pressures, Rising Competition, And Musk's Branding Crisis
data.description:
Are we seeing the fall of Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) or the fall of Elon Musk, or is it the other way around?

The truth is that the two things are connected, as the company is going through one of the most serious crises in its history, which has already caused its market value to fall by half in just a few months.

And yes, arguably, market correction and stretched valuations are the key factors behind this. But it's also important to make it clear that many of the factors that are causing the stock to plummet now are quite different from other moments in the company's history.

And that's what I believe investors need to understand, where I break down what's happening to Tesla into three major impacts, ranging from (1) structural changes in the global electric vehicle market; (2) competition with Chinese automakers; and (3) the last of which is linked to the company's leadership, its CEO Elon Musk.

The confluence of these three factors, in my opinion, tends to drive Tesla into the abyss, where it may be very difficult for robotaxi, FSD, and robotics to rescue it—especially in the short to medium term, at the very least.

While it seems intriguing and perhaps even somewhat paradoxical, I believe that as long as Elon Musk continues to repel Tesla's customers, and consequently destroy the reputation of the Tesla brand, the stock is likely to reflect it underperforming the markets.

For there to be a reversal of this bearish trend, perhaps turning Tesla into a SaaS (software-as-a-service) company as soon as possible could be crucial, and could perhaps be the catalyst needed for a turnaround. But even so, it's hard not to see this as a gamble at the moment, and I wouldn't particularly rely on it alone, considering that the EV business will arguably remain the major driving force of the company's top and bottom line as other AI initiatives are implemented over the next few years.

1. Global Structural Challenges Facing the EV Market

The first negative impact for Tesla has to do with the electric vehicle market itself.

By 2025, the world's perception of ISG, climate change, and fossil fuels have changed a lot, and with it the expectation that there would be a complete electrification of mobility, especially vehicles.

This scenario is no longer a reality or is no longer the expectation of consumers, governments, and even many automakers who, in recent months or years, have abandoned those projects that would change all their products to only electric or hybrid cars.

Some automakers have gone off the rails or projects to do away with combustion-powered products have been revived. This scenario has changed, and that idea of electric vehicle domination is behind us. That's not to say that the market is going to disappear, far from it, it will still be an important market, but not with the expectation or projection of dominance that it had just a few months ago. So this is the first impact, but not the main one.

2. Chinese Competition in the EV Landscape

The second negative impact, which is even more important than the first, has to do with competition from automakers in the electric vehicle market.

This is where Tesla is feeling it in several markets, especially in China, because there are many very competitive automakers offering much cheaper products— including some more technological ones—especially as is the case with BYD (OTCPK:BYDDY). It's worth noting that recently, the Chinese company also announced an important technological advance in battery charging, supposedly just 5 minutes, then a supercharger to charge the battery, and this competition, the technological innovation, doesn't stop.

And many of these automakers are taking important market shares. In underpenetrated markets like Latin America, the Chinese EV makers have taken over. Not yet in Europe. In the United States too, not least because of customs barriers. But this means that Tesla is suffering in several of these markets where, while it leads especially in premium cars, it doesn't have a low-cost electric car and is losing market share.

BYD has overtaken Tesla in sales, in total revenue, and should continue to gain more market share as it evolves in global markets. See the chart below illustrating Tesla's revenue growth versus BYD over the past five years. The same applies in terms of efficiency, as BYD has also managed not only to close the high operating margin gap it had versus Tesla over the past few years but to surpass them, with very solid management incorporating: (1) a vast diversification of models; (2) ever lower production costs; (3) economies of scale, with demand for EVs in China growing very strongly; and not least (4) subsidies from the Chinese government.

And Tesla even has an older product line, which is one of the criticisms of those in the EV market, that there isn't a very new vehicle to offer consumers. So yes, the competition from electric cars today is already hurting Tesla, especially from China.

3. The Politician CEO: Elon Musk's Dual Role

The third negative impact, which is, I would say, the main one, is really intriguing because it has to do with its main shareholder, its CEO, and its great leader, Elon Musk.

What was once a major asset and thus a risk for the company has now become a liability.

It's amazing how Musk's association with the right, especially since 2022, and now with the Trump administration, with supposedly the extreme right, with freedom of speech, and various other reasons, has led to Musk's image hurting Tesla.

And this is quite ironic considering that Musk has always had a leading role and carried many admirers, where he even had the idea that there was a sect of Tesla worshippers who bought vehicles for reasons such as protecting the environment, bought Tesla stock regardless of valuations, and fundamentals and revered the CEO as a revolutionary leader.

In short, it used to be a status to own a Tesla, and now it's becoming the opposite.

Musk's Anti-Woke Motivations Explained

To try to understand how this change in Elon Musk came about, I believe it all starts with Musk's own perception of the progressive 'woke' agenda.

According to Musk, 'woke' is a virus that needs to be exterminated, and he relies on a particularly traumatic experience with one of his children to have motivated him to be an avid opponent of this agenda. This is perhaps the first explanation for the change in Musk's behavior as well.

The second reason is also related to the 'woke' ideology and is freedom of expression.

Musk has been observing what is happening with social networks, with censorship, with the Big Techs, the big tech companies, especially on the big social networks, until he decided to buy Twitter, now the X platform, in 2022, as an attempt and with the aim of preserving freedom of expression. Those are his words.

And to prevent dissent from being silenced, to put an end to censorship, especially from the right, from conservatives, liberals, and libertarians, so that this platform, the internet, would be preserved as a space for debate, for the debate of ideas, which was no longer happening, in his assessment.

That was the main objective for buying Twitter, which in fact happened.

Musk and Tesla's Ties to the Republican Party

And since then, Elon Musk has become much more politically active.

This is undeniable, as he has become increasingly involved in more issues, especially in relation to freedom of expression. He has highlighted various episodes of censorship, of dissidents being persecuted, sometimes for mere opinions, persecuted by politics in various countries around the world. And as this unfolded and this reality became clearer in Musk's view, he felt that he needed to be more present in politics itself, until it came to the 2024 US presidential election.

And not only did he start supporting Trump, but he also donated hundreds of millions of dollars, and became the most important figure, President Trump's greatest influencer, to the point of taking part in the campaign, being present on stages, at rallies, giving speeches, and ended up being the great right-hand man and perhaps the main personality supporting President Trump.

And, without a doubt, his presence, and Musk's behavior, were decisive for the final result of the election.

Because of all this, Musk, of course, ended up in the middle of the road and, as continues to happen, collected many enemies.

Those who used to be on the other side of the political spectrum, more to the left, more progressive, the liberals in the U.S., began to see Musk in a different light, until Trump took office and created the famous Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE), putting Elon Musk in charge of this project, to reduce public spending, reduce waste, fraud, abuse, etc.

But it's a fact that what Musk has done, which, I believe, is excellent, is a great example for the rest of the world, of really reducing waste and cutting out what isn't needed. As he carries out all this promise with the DOGE, he is, in the meantime, antagonizing more and creating even more enmity, and not just because of the content he is doing because in the end he is cutting spending and, or he is instructing and recommending the Trump administration to cut spending, he is cutting agencies.

The DOGE has already announced that it's going to cut half the staff, and it might cut even more. So there are a lot of people who are, at the very least, disgusted or furious with what Elon Musk is doing at DOGE.

Musk’s Impact on Share Price and Market Sentiment

To better illustrate the damage to Tesla's value in 2025, the Nasdaq index fell 17% and Tesla fell 42%.

And, of course, from the all-time high that was reached right after President Trump's victory, Tesla's stock reached over $460. Since then, it has fallen by almost 50%. It's not the worst fall from an all-time high. In 2022, it fell by almost 75%, but what's striking now is the speed of that fall.

And that's why the current moment is so striking, compared to other Tesla periods, when there was also a downturn in which people sold the stock short.

Today, the short interest (around 2.9% of the float) of those who are selling the company's shares, is close to its lows. But still remain on the highest levels since early last year and way higher than back a few years ago.

There used to be a lot of disbelief about the business model and that Tesla could go bust, which almost happened in 2018. But at that time, Tesla's main customers, main investors, the base that supported the business, sales, and the company's own valuation, were supporting and embracing Elon Musk.

Now it's the other way around. Musk has managed to antagonize his main customers and investors, who are the most progressive, most to the left of the political spectrum, and not who he is allying himself with today.

And that's Musk's big dilemma because looking at the company's metrics, the valuation multiples is still quite stretched. Looking at the price over earnings of the share going forward, the projection is 90x, that's a lot.

So now there are several obstacles ahead. That's why investors see Tesla losing sales, which have already fallen by 2024. Deliveries from 2022 to 2023 rose sharply, by 1.8 million vehicles, but from 2023 to 2024 they fell slightly. The trend in 2025 is towards a further drop in market share.

Tesla's own report, its latest, which was released, also shows the Tesla market losing market share in the United States, losing it in Europe. It's still growing in China, but that's its smallest market and where it has its main competition. So it really is a delicate moment for the company. And because its valuation depends on this expectation of electric sales with a growing margin, it has almost been forced to reduce and discount the prices of its vehicles, which squeezes margins.

Before anyone argues that I'm not going to comment on robotaxis, FSD, and robotics, it's clear that Tesla's bulls expect that much, or virtually all, of Tesla's top and bottom-line growth will come from the sale of these new technologies.

However, although I won't go into these topics in depth, we can soon see that, according to some experts, Tesla is not necessarily at the same advanced level as Waymo, or other Chinese automakers. In other words, it is still debatable whether there is a clear superiority in Tesla's product.

Lastly, there is the possibility that in the future it will also dominate the robotics market with its robot, Optimus, which for the time being is not a final product, it is still being developed, but in the same way, the competition and the Chinese companies have also emerged and are trying to steal the market for this future product, which none of them really have ready to take over the world's homes.

It's important to note, however, that Tesla's valuation has almost always been based on future expectations. Some things have been delivered, others have not. Others have been postponed or continue to be postponed.

But if before there was a legion of satisfied customers, devotees, admirers, investors and willing to buy the paper, save, buy the products, and bet on the future, today this is changing. It's changing because of Musk's own attitude and behavior in recent months.

A Reverse DCF Analysis and the Margin of Safety

To better express how much would be needed for there to be some margin of safety in Tesla's investment thesis at current prices, I believe an interesting exercise would be to perform a reverse discounted cash flow (DCF) analysis to assess how realistic Tesla's share price really is.

For this analysis, I will consider a baseline for the next five years based on: (1) Tesla's D&A-to-CapEx ratio stays at 47%, and CapEx represents 12% of revenues, in line with the figures reported in the last twelve months; (2) conservatively, I will assume no changes in net working capital.

Furthermore, considering that the Wall Street consensus expects Tesla to grow revenues at a CAGR of 23% over the next five years. If I assume a discount rate of 9%, assuming a cost of equity of 8.8%, Tesla would need to report 37% in operating margins over the next five consecutive years, starting in 2025, for the company to reach an equity value of ~$823 billion—which divided between the 3.2 billion outstanding shares, would take Tesla to an implied price of ~$255 per share.

While this margin expansion is not unrealistic for some, if Tesla's Full Self-Driving (FSD) initiative could transform the company into a software-as-a-service (SaaS) business and achieve 80% margins on robotaxis—as even suggested by tech investor Cathie Wood—I think this is still speculative, and it's very unlikely that this will be the case for at least the next few years.

Therefore, I find no material evidence to suggest that there is any margin of safety in the share price of Tesla stock from this point of view.

Upside Risks to the Bearish Thesis

Elon Musk's track record as a public figure shows his resilience in the face of several periods of extreme hardship in his multiple businesses. Eventually, his involvement in politics may in fact accelerate regulatory advances around FSD, and with it, facilitate the implementation of robotaxis as quickly as possible on the streets of the U.S.—rapidly generating an expansion in the company's margins as an AI company.

If Musk's involvement in politics is indeed a move towards this goal, it could be a major home run for Tesla investors.

The Bottom Line

To what extent is Elon Musk willing to go ahead with the DOGE, making more enemies, and antagonizing his main clients and investors in order to carry out this mission to recover the U.S. economy with a zero deficit, to cut spending? How far is he willing to let Tesla bleed, which could damage not only the company, but his own personal fortune, and which could contaminate other businesses as well?

These are the dilemmas Musk faces today.

It is a great characteristic of Elon Musk's personality to get involved in controversy, to drag himself through the mud, and to sink deeper and deeper until he reaches rock bottom, in a situation that is sometimes almost irrecoverable. He's been involved in controversies throughout his public history over the last fifteen years, and at various times he's had to be helped by friends, by people he trusts, because it's a problem with his personality.

So I don't believe he won't go all the way. Even if that means Tesla losing another billion dollars in valuation. That's how he's managed to get his companies off the ground, avoid bankruptcy, and make them thrive.

Now, for those who are investing in Tesla, I would say that there is now a much bigger and more uncertain question mark than there was in the past. Although it's a point of debate for many, especially regarding Tesla's development as an AI company around FSD and robotics, I definitely don't find this not-so-new version of Elon Musk as a politician beneficial—especially associated with the Trump administration.

I also believe that, even if Tesla manages to deliver its new products such as the robotaxi in the not-so-distant future with no major delays, it will continue to struggle to win over consumers if the Tesla brand continues to be compromised by Elon Musk's image.

Therefore, as things stand, I don't believe that Tesla will be able to post substantial growth rates in revenues and margins in a short period of time (I'd say one to two years) that could justify Tesla as a Buy with an adequate margin of safety.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The Bond Market Is Sending Us A New Warning
data.description:
Introduction

My regular readers will know that I follow the bond market regularly, and have written explicitly on its patterns in the past, especially as it pertains to the ongoing market narratives and the next Fed decision.

The last time I spoke about the bond market as a primary indicator for the economy, it was regarding the 10yr US Treasury Yield (US10Y), and its place in the yield curve.

The yield curve is just one of the ways that we can visualize the spreads on US Treasuries, which tells us how investors are pricing in the future. Essentially, we plot the various rates of treasuries with different maturities. Using this, we can see what investors believe rates will be at a given time in the future, signalling their beliefs in the future of the economy and markets along with it.

For reference, here is how a normal, healthy yield curve is supposed to look:

Typically, if current rates are low (which has been the norm over the last two decades), we should see a gradual increase in yields over time as investors price in more and more "time uncertainty," or the risk that something will happen that causes rates to rise in the future. The more time a treasury has left (further out on the "maturity" axis), the more chance of something adverse happening.

However, when rates are elevated from historical norms (as they are presently), the yield curve starts to get weird.

The Yield Curve

Here is our current curve, with the Federal Funds Rate shown as a highlighted band, but for reference, it is 4.25-4.50%.

The market is currently forecasting that short-term rates are higher than they will be in the medium-term, but lower than they will be in the long-term, with rates at the following points as of 4/10/25 (time of writing).

Fed Funds 4.25%
3mo 4.34%
6mo 4.17%
2yr 3.84%
5yr 4.04%
10yr 4.40%
20yr 4.90%
30yr 4.86%

One of the things that is so startling is the insidious nature of the changes in the curve. The last time I wrote directly on the yield curve was back in late December. Here is the change between then and now. Keep in mind that the Federal Funds Rate has not changed in that time.

Note that the short-mid term maturities, namely the 2yr-7yr, have moved dramatically. Even the 10yr bond is now yielding less than it was just several months ago.

Here is a chart showing the probabilities for different rates from the Fed. Notice how we're not at an 80% chance the rates are held even at the next meeting in May. It seems that even though the bond market is pricing in future cuts, and at a faster rate than before, the futures market is pricing in no rate cuts yet.

I think this is a prudent move by the Fed, for the record.

The Bear Steepener

Flashback to my last article on the 10yr yield, here's the best way I can describe a "Bear Steepener":

There are a few major takeaways to note about a bear steepener:

  • Market phenomenon where long-term yields rise faster than short-term
  • Usually indicative of high inflationary expectations
  • Caused by investors selling long-term bonds more than short-term bonds

This is due to the anticipation of much higher rates into the future. Here is the spread between the 10yr and 2yr bonds, showing the velocity of the change...

Before we look at our current chart, let's take a look at where we came from. Here is the chart I showed in that article back in late December:

Since then, it's gotten worse. Here is the updated chart to today, showing that while yields retreated some after that quick change I called out in December, they've run up again and are now far above where they were before, as seen in the changes in the yield curve and illustrated here:

The cause of a bear steepener is normally due to the Fed raising rates too slowly, and the market anticipating that their efforts won't be enough in the long run to tamp down inflation, causing the spreads to vary so widely.

However, the Fed isn't raising rates right now. It's held the same rate since December, and is on track to lower rates this year (although I think fewer than the market is predicting).

The market is predicting rate cuts at a faster and faster rate, indicating that it is pricing in an economic downturn. This tracks with the ongoing tariff chaos that has whipsawed markets in recent days, and is causing recession fears I am on the record of calling "overblown."

Despite how I feel, other players in the market are far more concrete about their recession predictions.

  • JPMorgan Model Shows Recession Fear in Markets Spiking Up to 79%
  • Goldman Sachs Sees 65% Chance of US Recession in Next Year

Add to this that now the bond market itself is pricing this in at a faster and faster rate means that we may be closer than I anticipated. I am reserving the right to change my mind, of course, but I haven't yet. For now, this panic still feels like another fleeting episode in our new volatility regime.

Expect the VIX to stay elevated, and fluctuate greatly over the next few months at least. We've already touched 52+ in the past week, a level we haven't seen since the COVID-19 crash and the GFC before that.

What Am I Doing About It?

I have been piling all of my new capital into cash positions and commodities strategies, and have even been reducing some of my equity exposure. I began this process on March 31st, after publishing this article on Trump's "Liberation Day."

To borrow from that article, since my strategy hasn't changed since I wrote that, here is how I'm positioning myself for today's markets:

While I am not in "sell everything" mode, I have become very cautious around this situation. To that end, I am not currently deploying any new capital into equities or long bonds. Instead, I am currently sitting it in cash or alternatives.

I have recently recommended a cash-like fund that I personally own, the 0-3 Month Treasury Bond ETF (SGOV). This fund is very conservative, as it is designed to be a cash park. Cash is king right now, and the fund is currently paying me a forward yield of 4.2% to wait for the market to be done with its tantrum.

The other recommendation that I am actively buying right now is the Simplify Managed Futures ETF (CTA). This fund is effectively a manager, in this case Altis Partners sub-advises the fund, trading commodities to try to achieve total return. Through a variety of trading methods, they work to build positive track records regardless of market conditions. While they usually underperform during bull runs in stocks, these funds have historically performed best during times of uncertainty like now.

While CTA has not performed very well since I recommended it, it has still absolutely outperformed the index itself and equities in general since then. The losses seem largely to come from commodities sell-offs, as well as sell-offs in US treasuries (leading the bear steepener steepening) and Canadian treasuries. I still believe that the fund will outperform the market over the medium-term, and believe it to be the best investment in our current market environment until all the tariff business is settled.

Here is CTA against the market YTD:

Conclusion

Ultimately, we are seeing a worsening of expectations from the bond market, leading to a new warning for investors in the form of a deeper "bear steepener," a technical pattern we can illustrate with the dramatic widening of the 10yr and 2yr US Treasuries. The velocity of this change is worsening, and indicating that investors expect a recession in the US at a much higher probability than they did when I discussed the last time we saw this warning, back in December.

Now, investors are faced with a tough choice on how to proceed in markets that are as volatile as 2020 or 2008, and the bond market seems to be pricing in deep cuts to the central rate coming soon. While I don't share the bond market's direct views, believing that the Fed will act more prudently than expected, and is willing to tolerate more market pain than the market itself believes, I am not buying equities.

Instead, I am currently pushing cash into commodities strategies via Simplify's CTA and cash, waiting for the chaos to subside and the economic picture for the US to become more clear. Patience is prudent currently, as even the labor market and inflation data indicate that the Fed has no reason to cut rates.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: VTWG ETF: Go Elsewhere For Small-Cap Growth Exposure
data.description:
What is VTWG?

The Vanguard Russell 2000 Growth ETF (NASDAQ:VTWG) is a passively managed exchange-traded fund (also known as ETF) that invests in small-cap American listed stocks. The point of this ETF is to have a cheap, passively managed option to track outperforming stocks within the Russell 2000 Growth Index, with the fund manager rebalancing and reviewing the fund quarterly or less for investment opportunities.

VTWG's benchmark target is the Russell 2000 Growth Index and this article will tell you whether it's worth investing with VTWG or another asset manager/fund when thinking about investing in small cap equities. The underlying benchmark generally includes U.S. small-capitalization companies that have growth qualities to allow investors to try to outperform the larger small-cap bucket. Meanwhile, passive investing ETF's mean that the fund managers don't make many trades, and only rebalance every once in a while, versus active managers, who have the mandate to buy and sell stocks more often if they feel that there's a compelling investment opportunity. I've worked in capital markets credit risk in the fund business at one of the largest banks in Canada, and have found that more often than not, these actively managed ETFs are simply not worth the high fees.

After further review, my analysis shows that VTWG is not worth an investment vs. other passively managed peers. The fund has underperformed other passively managed and low fee Vanguard small-cap ETF's over the short and long term. I recommend investors buy the Vanguard Small-Cap Growth ETF (VBK), which has a different benchmark of small-cap U.S. equity or the Vanguard Small-Cap ETF (VB), which tracks a broad subset of small-caps but with more liquidity and less portfolio turnover, if investors want passively managed, low fee options with higher returns. I also wrote about VIOG recently, which tracks the S&P Small-Cap 600 Growth Index, and even that has better returns than VTWG.

Fund Breakdown

VTWG was launched on September 20, 2010, by Vanguard with a mandate to " track the performance of a benchmark index that measures the investment return of small-capitalization growth stocks in the United States". The Fund employs an indexing investment approach designed to track the performance of growth companies within the Russell 2000 Growth Index. The mandate makes sense, but I'm a bit dubious of these passively managed funds that try to find "growth" or "value" within these larger distinct buckets (i.e. small cap, mid cap, etc.). If you look at the returns on many of these funds, VTWG included, there isn't always a link to see the risk vs. reward returns that are mentioned in the prospectus strategy as the target.

That aside, the fund has been trading for years, with a NAV of ~$1.1Bn and a daily volume of over 31,000 shares traded. The idea of the fund isn't bad - the portfolio turnover is around 29%, so the managers are somewhat active. But upon further review of the ETF's results, I'm skeptical that the fund has fully achieved what it set out to do. Below is a chart over the past 5 years (since launch) showing returns slightly below other passively managed peers with similar low fee structures. VIOG, which I'm not a huge fan of anyway, has actually beaten VTWG easily, and it lets investors know if you want small-cap exposure, look toward narrower value subsets because taking a basket of thousands of small caps has led to lower alpha and too many duds that negatively impact returns.

Previous Results & Fund Portfolio

The fund employs a 0.15% yearly ETF expense ratio for fees to the asset manager (as of Dec. 31/24), similar and negligible to VBK and VIOG. Ultimately, the fund is ok, but I don't think it's set up as an outperformer based on its previous results and history, and that's clear in the Morningstar profile that shows a higher standard deviation and beta, leading to higher negative alpha than the index. Additionally, VTWG's Sharpe ratio for the fund is negative and well below the score of 1+ to prove strong risk-adjusted returns.

When comparing VTWG's portfolio to peers, there are subtle differences in the portfolio breakdown, but not many. As of Dec. 31, 2024, the portfolio mostly resembles the index benchmark but differences emerge with a higher industrial weighting and lower financialweights, which makes sense given the r-squared value of just 75.4. With the Federal Reserve taking a wait and see approach to rate cuts given upcoming tariffs, small-cap stocks may be left in limbo, after the last few years of underperformance due to higher borrowing costs. Given VTWG's underperformance vs. peers, I'd rather buy VBK of VB if I expect a rally in small-caps to occur.

Risks & Bottom Line

The bottom line for this fund, in my opinion, is that the juice is not worth the squeeze. There's simply not enough good news to justify this "growth" small cap fund, and investors can simply get normal small cap exposure through VB, or VBK. VTWG doesn't have enough differentiation and hasn't outperformed to convince me it's a better ETF alternative than other passive counterparts. With risks of small-caps being hit hard by tariffs and with the fund having more volatility than the broader index, less liquidity, and with other accessible options, I think investors can look elsewhere away from VTWG when seeking small-cap equity exposure.

data.title: Meta Platforms: Back To $750
data.description:
Meta Platforms, Inc. (NASDAQ:META) slumped to $500 recently on global trade war fears, but the social media company doesn't really face a lot of direct tariff impacts. The stock had previously soared to nearly hit the $750 target for this year, and a rebound should be in the works. My investment thesis is ultra-Bullish on the stock in the mid-$500 range following the wild trading this week.

Limited Tariff Impact

Meta runs a social media network focused on advertising not directly impacted by the global trade war. The biggest impact to the company would be depressed ad demand with customers impacted by tariffs.

The advertisement market has most likely felt some disruption as businesses halt plans due to uncertain tariffs. According to data provided by marketing analytics firm WARC, growth forecasts for advertising spend have been downgraded by 0.9 percentage points to 6.7% in 2025 and 0.7 percentage points to 6.3% in 2026, equivalent to a nearly $20 billion cut in overall ad revenues.

According to James McDonald, author of WARC’s report and director of data intelligence and forecasting, the digital ad market remains strong, but the overall market will take a hit for the following reasons:

The global ad market faces mounting uncertainty as trade tariffs, economic stagnation, and tightening regulation disrupt key sectors, leading us to cut growth prospects by $20 billion over the next two years. Automakers, retailers, and tech brands, in particular, are now reining in ad spend amid rising manufacturing costs and mounting supply chain pressures.

Meta just completed a big year, with 2024 revenues soaring 22% to reach $165 billion. The social media company already faced a year where AI capex spending was becoming a bigger issue.

The company guided to spending $60 to $65 billion on capex this year. With 2026 revenue forecasts at $188 billion, the company guided to spending ~33% of revenues on capex.

Since Alphabet Inc./Google (GOOG) just reaffirmed plans on spending $75 billion on capex this year, the global AI race remains in full force, and Meta is unlikely to cut spending. The social media company produced $54 billion in free flow last year, despite spending $37 billion on capex during 2024.

As the historical numbers appear to highlight, Meta boosts free cash flows generally at a similar rate as investing in capital goods. Remember, the company is heavily spending on servers with useful lives of 4 to 6 years, so any spending today will continue contributing to revenues for the rest of the decade.

Meta generated an incredible $91 billion in operating cash flows in 2024. Also, the company had net income of $62 billion to slightly exceed the FCF due to lower depreciation expenses than capex spending.

The company will face an even bigger impact going forward as capex growth of over $20 billion this year eats into FCF, but doesn't impact profits as much. Meta is predicted to boost revenues by a substantial amount in the next 4 years, with revenues going from $165 billion last year to nearly $100 billion higher by 2028.

The big revenue upside is partly due to the progress Meta has made with AI. The company has improved advertising metrics due to the LLAMA gen AI models.

The main reason to turn negative about Meta is the point where additional capex spending on AI doesn't lead to higher revenue to warrant the higher spending. For now, though, the company has a history of turning higher investments into better results.

EPS Boost – Ad Weakness

Meta continues to have the opportunity to boost EPS via cutting Reality Labs spending. The company will need to show some improvement in these financials at some future point, considering revenues only grew $150 million last year to reach $2.1 billion.

As Meta promised all year, the company was going to continue spending more on developing the Metaverse and smart glasses of the future. The company lost nearly $18 billion on the business for the year.

With 2.6 billion shares outstanding and a 15% tax rate, Meta is taking a nearly $6 EPS hit by investing this level into building smart glasses and the Metaverse. Of course, smart glasses have long been promoted as the product with the potential to replace smartphones, with a global market opportunity of ~$600 billion in 2025.

Similar to capex, Meta is spending aggressively to build large market opportunities. All while, the company is targeted to produce a $25 EPS this year and reach nearly $29 in 2026.

Our view continues to hold, investors should buy the stock based on the adjusted EPS adding back the Reality Labs losses. Meta would produce an EPS approaching $35 in 2026, making the stock absurdly cheap at $550 and still only trading at 16x adjusted EPS targets.

The company will report Q1 results on April 30 and investors should expect mixed results. The current EPS estimates are $5.21 for nearly 11% growth YoY.

As has been the case for a while, Meta could easily benefit from a TikTok ban. Instagram would likely see a solid boost in usage and more ad revenues from TikTok getting banned. Considering China is the one country outlined in the ongoing tariff wars with a 145% tariff hike, the TikTok ban could easily go into place.

Meta likely only sees some short-term blips on ad demand. The stock is a buy down here around $500 on any demand-driven weakness, looking for a rebound to $750.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Meta Platforms is attractively priced again. Investors should expect some blips in advertising demand hitting short-term results, but the stock is cheap based on the prospects for boosting long-term results via strong investments in the future.

data.title: Manulife: Should You Buy The Dip? (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
As I've covered in previous articles, while I like Manulife Financial Corporation (NYSE:MFC) business profile and good growth prospects over the long term, I was cautious on its valuation over the past few months. Indeed, due to a positive operating performance in the recent past, its share price has been on a positive trend, leading to a valuation at the top of its historical range, measured by the price-to-book value multiple.

As shown in the previous graph, Manulife's historical multiple has been around 1.1x book value over the past five years, while in recent months it was trading between 1.6-1.7x book value. This was a key reason why my recommendation changed to "hold" back in November, as I saw upside as somewhat limited at this relatively high valuation.

Having said that, the company has made several efforts to improve its fundamentals in recent years, namely by using reinsurance transactions to reduce its exposure to long-term care (LTC) insurance, which was one of the main weak points of its investment case. This largely justifies a higher valuation multiple, plus the company has also reported a positive operating performance lately, as I analyzed a couple of months ago related to its 2024 earnings.

Moreover, the company seems to be quite confident about its business prospects in the near future, and raised its quarterly dividend by 10%, which was well received by investors. However, Manulife's share price was naturally impacted more recently by the market turmoil following Trump's tariffs, potentially creating a buying opportunity for long-term investors.

Indeed, its shares are currently trading slightly lower than 1.5x book value following a share price correction of more about 15% in recent weeks, which is now within my target range of 1.4-1.5x multiple as I've analyzed in my last article on Manulife. While Manulife is not much impacted directly by tariffs, the company is significantly exposed to Asia. Thus, it may be impacted indirectly by lower economic growth in the future as Trump raised tariffs across Asian countries. Additionally, increased market volatility is also negative for its investment portfolio, justifying to some extent some share price weakness.

However, its exposure to equities is not concerning given that public equities only represented about 8% of its investment portfolio at the end of 2024, while the majority of its investments are allocated to low-risk asset classes, such as government and corporate bonds, mortgages, and private debt.

This is a conservative investment allocation and regarding specific exposures, the vast majority of its bond portfolio is rated A or higher, while about 96% were investment grade bonds. This shows that Manulife's risk approach is also conservative, and the company does not take much credit risk, being a positive factor during periods of market turmoil like the one experienced in recent weeks.

Regarding its earnings sensitivity to market swings, the company does not have much exposure to changes in interest rates or credit spreads, with a 50 basis points (bps) adverse movement impacting its net income by about CAD 100 million both to rising rates and widening spreads, while a 10% drop on the S&P 500 index is expected to impact its bottom-line by about CAD 230 million after taxes. Given that its net income was nearly CAD 5.4 billion in 2024, these impacts seem to be manageable and doesn't make Manulife much exposed to capital markets, like most companies in the insurance sector.

Regarding its capital ratio, the impact of market swings is higher because beyond the earnings impact there is also the fair value of securities that is reflected on other comprehensive income. Its LICAT ratio was 137% at the end of 2024, being comfortably above the regulator's requirement, and according to Manulife's disclosures its capital ratio would be impacted by about three percentage points if credit spreads declined by 50 bps, while the impact of lower equities is likely below ten percentage points. While this is not concerning given the company's good profitability and capital generation capacity, recent market turmoil can have some impact on its capital ratio and, potentially, negatively impact its capital returns ahead. I think the most likely option, if the company decides to be more conservative regarding capital returns, is to pause share buybacks, as repurchase of own shares has a more discretionary approach compared to dividends.

Given this background, while there is some justification for a lower share price in recent weeks due to the company's exposure to capital markets, the impact seems to be manageable and, in my opinion, don't justify to a full extent Manulife's recent share price correction.

From an operating perspective, Manulife's main growth engine has been Asia in the recent past, a region that is being targeted by Trump's tariffs in a significant way.

Across the region, the most important markets are Hong Kong, Japan, mainland China, and Singapore, while Asia contributed to 34% of the company's core earnings and about 12% of its assets under management and administration. Moreover, its Annual Premium Equivalent (APE) sales in 2024 were mainly generated in Asia, accounting for some 72% of total APE sales in the year, showing that future growth is highly reliant on Asia.

While life insurance is not an industry that will be directly much affected by tariffs, it's indirectly exposed by the potential negative impact on economic growth across the region. The U.S. is a major trading partner for most Asian countries. Thus, higher tariffs will certainly have a negative impact on exports and general economic activity over the medium to long term, being a headwind for growth in the insurance industry.

As Manulife's growth prospects are largely dependent on Asia, investors are naturally concerned, and this is also a key reason why Manulife's share price has reacted badly to the recent tariff's drama. However, there are structural growth drivers for the insurance industry in the region that aren't expected to be much affected by U.S. tariffs, namely aging population, the rising middle-class wealth, and increasing penetration of the insurance industry. On the other hand, higher economic growth compared to other geographies may be at risk to some extent due to tariffs and its negative impact on trade, being still quite uncertain what the ultimate impact will be. Nevertheless, Asia is expected to remain a core driver of growth for Manulife, and this is not expected to change much despite higher geopolitical risk related to trade wars, as the business prospects in the region are quite good for life insurance companies like Manulife.

Moreover, investors should be aware that tariffs impact mainly P&C rather than life insurance companies, as tariffs are expected to lead to higher loss ratios for specific coverages, like auto or home insurance, potentially impacting the underwriting profitability of P&C carriers in the future.

This means Manulife's operating performance is not expected to be much impacted directly by tariffs and, theoretically, this could be a defensive play in the current uncertain market environment. However, there is potentially indirect impact on economic growth in Asia, lower investment gains related to its investment portfolio, and also some negative impact on its asset management unit. In recent quarters, Asia and Global Wealth and Asset Management (Global WAM) have been its major growth drivers, but during periods of market volatility clients usually tend to make withdrawals from investment products, which may lead to negative flows during the current quarter and impact its operating momentum in Global WAM. While insurance and its investment results are larger contributors to Manulife's core earnings, some slowdown in Global WAM earnings can be negative for the company's overall earnings momentum, being also a reason for some share price weakness recently.

Conclusion

Manulife has great fundamentals and good growth prospects and despite its business being indirectly impacted by Trump's tariffs, the negative impact seems to be manageable and doesn't change its investment case over the long term. Therefore, the recent share price correction seems to be a buying opportunity for long-term investors, as Manulife's valuation is now more attractive than it was some months ago and is also offering a higher dividend yield, making it an interesting play again in the life insurance sector.

data.title: UiPath: Ignore The Bearish Noise And Buy This AI Gem
data.description:
Authored by Winfred Mwiti and Jacques Georget

Investment Thesis

Since our last article on UiPath Inc. (NYSE:PATH), the stock has lost ~20%. But as you are all aware, this is due to a broader market trend with the S&P500 falling by ~13% for the same period.

We believe that the future for PATH remains bright. The company is making strides to adapt to the booming AI market, including a strategic pivot towards Agentic AI. Furthermore, the company boasts strong customer retention signaling a solid product offering coupled with great customer satisfaction. There is also a large and rapidly growing TAM for PATH. In addition, our valuation model indicates that the stock is trading at a significant discount, offering a decent entry point.

The Broader Tech Sell-Off & The Risk It Poses To UiPath

Among the major headlines in the world today is the significant turbulence experienced by tech stocks in recent months. For instance, the Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJI) is in a strong downtrend, recently losing more than 11% YTD.

The ongoing volatility has been driven by both macroeconomic and geopolitical factors, most notably the escalating global trade wars sparked by President Trump’s aggressive tariff policies. To demonstrate how the ongoing trade wars have catalyzed the ongoing volatility, the DJI plunged by more than 2,200 points in a single day recently following China’s retaliation against U.S. tariffs, signaling heightened economic uncertainty. PATH hasn’t been immune to this turmoil, with the stock declining more than 21% YTD.

While these trade wars are likely to persist in the near future, leading to a potential recession, we find three major risks which it poses to PATH. Firstly, we see a potential for reduced enterprise spending and PATH’s customer base being primarily large enterprises which rely heavily on discretionary budgets for automation could see tighter budgets, perhaps stagnating its ARR.

Additionally, the 25% tax proposal on goods from Canada and Mexico could increase the cost of hardware and infrastructure that PATH clients depend on for deploying automation solutions. For example, higher data center costs are a concern already for Big Tech AI ambitions, something that could trickle down to the UiPath ecosystem, making its solutions less cost competitive.

Lastly, we anticipate weak market sentiments if the current geopolitical climate persists. With the market having turned bearish already, we expect investors to be increasingly wary of the current momentum and despite its strong fundamentals which we shall discuss shortly; the weak market sentiment could exacerbate downward pressure on its stocks at least in the short run.

Nevertheless, despite these potential risks, we believe the company’s AI innovations and its fundamentals (market leadership and high customer retention) offer us solace.

PATH Still Leads In A High Growth Market

PATH is a dominant player in the RPA market, being a leader in robotic process automation. The RPA market is seeing a robust demand as businesses strive to streamline operations while reducing costs. Grand View Research estimates that the global RPA market will grow by a CAGR of 43.9% between 2025 and 2030.

Given the company’s dominant market position, leading with a 35.8% market share, UiPath is well-positioned to ride this strong market growth. Its integration of AI into its platform through capabilities such as ML, NLP and intelligent document processing give the company a competitive edge. Its end-to-end automation platform combining UI automation and API integration further cements its competitive advantage.

In our view, despite the deceleration in quarterly revenue growth over the last four quarters, this company’s consistent revenue growth, up 135% compared to 5-years ago, and its consistent ARR growth underscores the company’s ability to deliver irrespective of economic uncertainties.

Innovation: Pivoting To Agentic AI

One of the most compelling reasons we have for investing in PATH is its aggressive push into Agentic AI (the next-generation approach where AI agents autonomously perform complex tasks collaborating with humans and other systems). The company has introduced AI-powered products such as autopilot, agent builder, Agentic testing and Agentic orchestration. This evolution is positioning the company to compete in the rapidly growing AI market, which we expect to complement rather than replace its core RPA business model.

To demonstrate the impact of this shift, following the company’s launch of its Agent Builder Software in Dec 2024, it has garnered significant interest with more than 1,000 organizations signing up for private preview.

It is our view that this move addresses the concerns that generative AI could disrupt traditional RPA. With the quick numbers in the Agent Builder Software signaling the potential success of this new venture, it could reignite UiPath's growth trajectory as businesses increase adoption to AI-driven automation.

Why The Innovations Matter

In our view, we think that this company’s innovations offer a significant counterweight to the current challenges by positioning the company to weather the storms. To support this, we highlight two major aspects. To begin with, it will enhance its automation capabilities. Its recent innovation such as the Test cloud which leverages AI to streamline software testing, and the integration of the Agent Builder and Autopilot empowers enterprises to automate complex workflows such as those in SAP and Oracle environments. This addresses a critical point for businesses (faster innovation cycles) potentially making its offerings indispensable even during downturns.

Secondly, PATH has carved out a niche in enterprise automation through its recent innovations, potentially improving its growth potential. It is estimated that 90% of IT executives have processes that need Agentic AI to improve, with 77% being ready to invest in Agentic AI this year. This presents a tremendous growth opportunity for this company.

Customer Retention & Expansion

When we look at PATH’s customer metrics, we can see that its growth potential is anchored on a growing customer base. In its 2025 fiscal year, the company maintained a dollar-based net retention rate of 110%, an indication that its existing clients are not only staying but also spending more. Further, its number of high-value clients was on the rise. Its customers with $100,000 or more in ARR grew to 2,292 in Q4 2025 up from 2,054 in Q4 2024, with those with $1 million or more growing to 317 in Q4 2025 from 288 the previous year.

Above all, in the 2025 fiscal year, customers with $5 million or more in ARR saw a 30% growth. With this expansion and a total customer base of about 10,750, we think the company’s ability to deepen relationships with customers and grow its customer base has been demonstrated strongly.

Financial Projection & Valuation

Our financial projections for PATH assume a revenue growth rate of 6.8% in 2026 and 9% in 2027 respectively. The low growth rate for 2026 is reflective of the cautious outlook that management has for the year. It also reflects the recent decline in YoY revenue growth.

However, we have a more optimistic view for 2027 as we expect that the company's recent products will have started impacting its top line, while restoring its upward growth trajectory. We also expect improvements in PATH's EBITDA margin, driven by the company’s shift to its high-margin AI-driven product offerings.

In light of the above projections, we value this stock using the P/S valuation method while comparing PATH to some of its peers. From our comparative analysis, we find that PATH is trading at a significant discount. The peer group's average forward PS ratio is 7.68x, while PATH’s forward PS multiple sits at only 3.62. This disparity is not justified, particularly considering that PATH's 3-year revenue CAGR of ~17% is just a few percentage points below that of the peer group.

As such, we value this stock using a PS multiple of 7x, which is slightly below the peer group average, reflecting the upside potential from its share buybacks and growth initiatives such as the shift to Agentic AI. Assuming this multiple, we estimate a target price of ~$21 per share.

Share Buybacks to Boast the Stock Price

In FY 2025, PATH authorized $500 million for share buybacks, bringing the total to $554 million. Assuming its current stock price of ~$10, this program could repurchase approximately 49.8 million shares of the 551.49 million outstanding shares, approximately 9% of the outstanding shares. This could boost EPS by ~9%, making the stock more attractive to investors and supporting a higher valuation.

Looking at the balance sheet, this company has a strong liquidity given its cash balance of $1.63 billion. It then follows that the $500 million shares repurchase approval would leave the company with about $1.1 billion in cash reserve, which is ample liquidity to support both development and potentially expanding further on its share buyback program.

Risks

As with all investments, PATH has its risks as well. The shift towards generative AI raises questions as to whether RPA could be fully commoditized. Nevertheless, the company’s integration of AI into its platforms mitigates this threat and positions it as a hybrid player that stands between traditional automation and next-generation AI solutions. Economic uncertainty is another risk that we are seeing given the slowing global economic growth. The negative effect could be seen in the weak guidance for Q1 FY 2026.

Takeaway Message

PATH's fundamentals tell a very tempting story. The company is a market leader in RPA and can boast of a large and growing customer base. Furthermore, its efforts towards AI integration seems like the perfect bet and will help the company capture a major portion of its large and rapidly growing TAM. PATH also recently expanded on its share buyback program, which should give another push to the stock price. All in all, this translates into a unique buying opportunity. Considering the sharp correction in the stock price, this company is poised for a comeback.

data.title: Tilray: Sell Before Potential Reverse Split
data.description:
One of the worst performing names in the market continues to be Tilray (NASDAQ:TLRY). The Canadian cannabis and beverage firm has seen its shares plunge more than 92% over the past five years as revenue growth has not materialized as expected and significant cash burn has led to substantial dilution. On Tuesday, the company reported its fiscal third quarter results, which were not very good. Despite shares hitting a new multi-year low after the report, there still could be plenty of downside ahead.

Previous Coverage Of The Name

I last looked at Tilray back in mid-March, at which point short interest in the name had surged to a new high. As shares plunged and the company was swapping debt for equity, the number of shares short continued to rise. It didn't help that analysts were continuing to reduce their price targets, and fears over a US / Canada trade war were hurting overall economic sentiment.

Since I went to a sell rating on Tilray back in January, shares have lost about 57% of their value. US markets have declined a little over that time, but certainly not to that degree. I've warned recently that Tilray would likely need to reverse split its stock to maintain exchange listing requirements, and the chances of that have only increased since, with shares continuing to trade well below the $1.00 threshold they need to be at.

The Fiscal Q3 Report

Tilray's numbers are a bit messy at the moment as it looks to reorganize the business. Net revenues for Q3 came in at less than $186 million, or $193 million in constant currency. The net number was down a few million from the prior year period, a quarter that contained about $6 million of revenue from now discontinued items. Management noted that strategic initiatives and SKU rationalization impacted total revenue generated by $13.2 million in the current year quarter.

No matter which way you slice it, the number was a major disappointment, as the street was looking for almost $210 million for the period. Beverage revenue was up less than 3% year over year, hit hard by a $6 million impact from the SKU rationalization. Net cannabis revenues were down more than 14% year over year, impacted by currencies, the timing of moving certain product to international markets, and the decision to pause the company's presence in certain items such as vapes and infused pre-rolls.

The company did report a two percentage point increase in adjusted beverage margins, but adjusted cannabis margins fell by eight percentage points. This led to an overall one percentage point decrease in company-wide adjusted gross margins. The company swung to an adjusted loss of $2.9 million in the period from a $0.9 million profit a year earlier. However, this essentially resulted in a break-even loss per share, three cents better than the street was looking for. On a GAAP basis, the company lost nearly $800 million, mostly thanks to a roughly $700 million impairment charge.

Between currency issues and the SKU rationalization efforts, Tilray reduced its fiscal year revenue guidance by $50 million. The company is now looking for $850 million to $900 million. I noted previously how analysts were skeptical of the previously higher range, and that stance was partially reflected in the cut here today, but the new guidance midpoint is still well below the $892 million analyst average estimate that was seen entering Tuesday.

More Dilution Coming

One of the main issues I've had with Tilray over the years is its financial situation. Large losses have led to major cash burn and dilution. The company burned roughly $20 million in Q3, although this was an improvement of about $4 million over the prior year period. However, if we look at the company's adjusted free cash flow metric, the more than $18 million burn was a stark reversal from the $0.6 million generated in fiscal Q3 2024.

Tilray ended the February 2025 period with about $248 million in cash and marketable securities, and a little more than $277 million in total debt. The net debt number there did improve by about $28.7 million in the period, partially thanks to the debt for equity swaps I mentioned in my prior article. However, that meant that investors saw another round of dilution, with the share count hitting another new high as seen in the chart below.

At more than 983 million at the end of February, the number of shares outstanding here has basically doubled in three years. Back in December, the company received shareholder approval to increase its authorized share count from 1.208 billion to 1.426 billion. As a reminder, the company is not only swapping debt for equity at this time, but using an outstanding equity sales program to raise cash in most quarters. The outstanding share count will likely top 1 billion at some point this year, although the physical number could change dramatically if we do get a reverse split.

A Look At The Valuation Picture

On the valuation front, Tilray finished trading Thursday for about 0.56 times the current revenue expected for the May 2025 fiscal year. That's a meaningful discount to the valuation that peer Canopy Growth (CGC) went for at 0.69 times its March 2025 expected revenue. Tilray is projected to see more revenue growth in its current fiscal year, but Canopy is expected to show a little more when each gets to its next respective fiscal period. The gap in valuation between these two has narrowed quite a bit since I last looked at these two companies' price to sales numbers, but both have come down.

Perhaps surprisingly, street analysts are still extremely positive on Tilray. The average price target as seen in the graphic below is currently $1.33, which would imply more than 160% upside from current levels. I should note, however, that the street average was $2.60 a year ago and nearly $10 just three years ago, so analysts have continued to cut their targets over time and seemingly are just playing a game of catch up as the stock drops further.

My main negative catalyst to watch in the near term continues to be a potential reverse split, with the stock well under a dollar per share. I've covered numerous reverse splits over the years on this site, and usually, we get a leg down on the announcement and another once the reverse split actually happens. Barring some dramatic rally in shares, the only question now appears to be what the reverse split ratio will be.

The only way I could see shares surging in the near term is if this company somehow gets bought out. Perhaps a larger player in the beverage space thinks there is enough synergies from a merger to make this business work, or some private equity firm thinks it can reduce debt here and cut enough costs out over time. The only thing that makes this a little hard to see right now is that Tilray bought a lot of its beverage brands from larger firms that didn't seem to want them, and the cannabis business continues to struggle in the near term.

Final Thoughts And Recommendation

In the end, Tilray announced another set of disappointing results on Tuesday. Even excluding headwinds from some discontinued products, revenues came in well short of street estimates. While the adjusted loss wasn't as bad as expected, the company is still burning cash at a decent clip. The net debt balance only improved because investors were diluted meaningfully thanks to share sales and debt for equity swaps.

With a reverse split increasingly likely, I have to maintain my sell rating on the stock today. The company continues to disappoint when it comes to revenue growth, and it hasn't been able to deliver any sustained positive cash flow over time. For me to increase my rating moving forward, those two items have to show some decent improvement, and we have to see dilution show some signs that it will be leveling off. Even though shares have been hit hard in recent months, more new lows seem possible here.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: AMOM: The Experiment Has Failed For This AI-Powered Momentum ETF
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The QRAFT AI-Enhanced U.S. Large Cap Momentum ETF (NYSEARCA:AMOM) has been a huge disappointment since its launch six years ago, and with a high 0.75% expense ratio and just $23 million in assets under management, I don't expect this ETF to survive much longer. In addition to reviewing its performance and strongly urging shareholders to sell AMOM now, I'll highlight some historical and current fundamentals that help explain why AMOM has struggled, which I hope you can use to identify a more suitable momentum fund for the long run. I hope you enjoy the read.

AMOM Overview

Strategy Discussion

AMOM's strategy is proprietary, so we don't know exactly how QRAFT's AI is designed. However, an explanatory video and a general overview of its process on its website note the following:

1. The AI utilizes machine learning to explore "a vast database to narrow down valid momentum factor stocks" using macro, price, and fundamental data. This "Data Pre-Processing" phase aims to remove survivorship bias and look ahead bias and helps ensure the AI model "is accurate and in the right time frame."

2. The AI performs scenario analysis to test various factor combinations. In this "Factor Factory" phase, the AI aims to discover investment opportunities related to a company's quality, size, value, momentum, and low volatility. As a momentum ETF, AMOM focuses on the momentum factor, though it's unclear how many other factors count.

3. The AI constructs a portfolio of the top 50 stocks it believes can deliver stronger returns than conventional momentum investing strategies. In this "Strategy Factory" phase, the data is back- and forward-tested with training and test data.

4. The portfolio rebalances monthly to ensure consistent exposure to high-momentum stocks.

The last point is no joke, either. Not only does the portfolio rebalance monthly, but it essentially substitutes about half the stocks each time. As shown below, AMOM's portfolio turnover rate ranged between 346% and 790% between 2021 and 2024. Taking the average annual rate of 523%, that means roughly 45% of the portfolio is swapped each month on average.

With these high-turnover funds, it is crucial shareholders don't get tied to the actual holdings. Instead, they should focus on factor exposures instead. I mentioned in point #2 how it was unclear how well AMOM emphasized other non-momentum factors, but fortunately, I've kept pretty good records over the last year, and I should be able to shed some light on that unknown.

Performance vs. Peer Group

As I mentioned earlier, AMOM has a costly 0.75% expense ratio, and it's probably one reason for its low $23 million in assets under management. At this level, QRAFT is only bringing in about $175K in fees each year, which is hardly enough to support an ETF. That is why I believe it's a going concern, especially since it's almost six years old and there are many other alternatives available, including the four listed below.

  1. Invesco S&P 500® Momentum ETF (SPMO).
  2. iShares MSCI USA Momentum Factor ETF (MTUM).
  3. JPMorgan U.S. Momentum Factor ETF (JMOM).
  4. SEI Enhanced US Large Cap Momentum Factor ETF (SEIM).

Not only are these ETFs cheaper and more popular, but they've also delivered superior returns. I've excluded SEIM from the performance chart below due to its later launch date, but AMOM has lagged behind SPMO and JMOM since its inception on May 20, 2019.

It still has the edge over MTUM (105.46% vs. 89.64%), but it's remarkable how poorly it's performed since November 2021. As highlighted, its $19,884 growth on a $10K investment translated into a 99% gain to the end of October 2021, which means it's only added 6% in the last 3+ years. These results cast doubt that its AI is better than the conventional momentum strategies it seeks to outperform.

AMOM Fundamentals

Factor Ranks Since July 2024

Beginning in July 2024, I started compiling start-of-month factor rankings for nearly 1,000 U.S. Equity ETFs. These rankings provide a snapshot of the factors funds emphasize each month, which is important for high-turnover proprietary funds like AMOM. Take a look over the table below, and please note that a lower rank indicates AMOM had higher exposure to the factor.

Here are three quick takeaways:

1. AMOM consistently ranks well on the momentum factor, which confirms the two systems generally align. That's what we want to see from any momentum fund, and in the last 11 months, AMOM has also consistently scored well on the growth factor and low on the risk and size factors. That's normal. AMOM is a large-cap ETF, so I don't expect it to hold smaller companies, and higher volatility usually comes with the territory when investing in growth stocks.

2. Momentum and sentiment (earnings revisions, sales/earnings surprises) are normally highly correlated because stocks with strong price momentum usually have catalysts like good earnings reports or earnings upgrades. However, AMOM's sentiment rank has bounced around, which I captured in the "standard deviation" column on the right. Since AI doesn't seem to factor in these statistics, this increases the chance that shareholders could end up holding high-momentum stocks even after they've reported poor results or had their earnings outlook downgraded by Wall Street analysts.

3. AMOM's quality rank is inconsistent, ranging between #88 in November 2024 to #393 in March 2025. The fund's quality started to decline in January 2025 and remained poor up until the start of April, though as we'll see shortly, that's reversed again after this month's rebalancing. Still, the damage has been done. Since AMOM's quality rank dipped in January 2025, it's lagged behind SPMO by 12.86% YTD through April 9.

Current Fundamental Analysis: Top 25 Holdings

With these factors in mind, let's now look at how AMOM compares with SPMO, MTUM, JMOM, and SEIM fundamentally. The following table highlights selected statistics for its top 25 holdings, which comprise 73.95% of the portfolio.

This portfolio actually looks quite solid. AMOM's weighted average of 27.67% and 13.01% ROE and ROA figures are terrific and feed into its 9.39/10 sector-adjusted profit score, which I derived from Seeking Alpha Factor Grades. I don't see any major sentiment concerns, as its 7.01/10 EPS Revision Score ranks #2 behind SEIM's. It trades at a pretty expensive 24.79x forward earnings after Wednesday's historic gains, but it compensates investors with its 24.61% one-year estimated earnings growth rate, the best in the peer group. Lastly, AMOM's 1.15 five-year portfolio beta suggests that it might even be less risky than SPMO, even with 9.57% allocated to NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA).

However, readers must remember this is only a snapshot, and if history is any guide, these holdings will change completely in a matter of months. My main concern is a decline in quality led by stocks like MicroStrategy Incorporated (MSTR) and Robinhood Markets, Inc. (HOOD), which AMOM held just two months ago. If QRAFT could tighten this up and not allow such speculative companies into the fund, I believe results would improve.

Investment Recommendation

AMOM is an AI-powered large-cap momentum fund aiming to outperform conventional momentum ETFs like SPMO, MTUM, JMOM, and SEIM. Its 0.75% expense ratio is one drawback, but in my judgment, its main flaw is a lack of consistency emphasizing non-momentum factors like quality. Monthly portfolio turnover approaches 50% per month on average, and although AMOM looks fundamentally strong today, I have no confidence that will be the case moving forward. As a result, AMOM is a single-factor fund that's destined to deliver inconsistent returns, as illustrated by Morningstar's quartile and percentile rankings below.

Even more concerning is AMOM's $23 million in AUM amassed over the last six years. Given the costs of running an ETF, I doubt AMOM is profitable, so I wouldn't be surprised to see it liquidate soon. Therefore, I recommend owners "sell" AMOM today and consider one of the four alternatives presented today. JMOM and SEIM rank high on my end, but if you'd like more information on another momentum fund not mentioned today, please feel free to ask in the comments section below. Thank you for reading.
